Lead promotional messaging with an athlete partnership (a current Olympian on the box), sample at athletic events, and place in the cereal aisle. Year-one success metric: 25% of buyers purchasing a second box within 60 days.

keyTerms.definition: Retail distribution coverage metric — General Mills' 92% ACV means Wheaties Protein is available in stores representing 92% of all grocery sales in the US, a massive advantage over Magic Spoon's DTC-only reach
The percentage of first-time buyers who purchase the product again; in cereal, a repeat rate below 20% at 60 days typically signals a taste or value-perception problem
Launching a new product under an existing brand name to leverage its equity; Wheaties Protein is a brand extension that borrows the 'Breakfast of Champions' equity to enter the protein cereal segment
The key functional product claim; 20g of protein per serving is the threshold that signals 'meaningful' protein content to fitness-oriented consumers who typically target 25-30g per meal
A positioning strategy where a product is priced above the commodity category but well below the premium DTC leader — Wheaties Protein at $6.99 vs. Magic Spoon at $13.99 occupies this middle ground

interviewerExpectations: This is an 'easy' case, so the bar is a clean, well-structured STP with a specific positioning decision and a direct recommendation. Interviewers want to see the Magic Spoon comparison handled explicitly and the distribution advantage called out as the key strategic lever. Weak answers are generic ('target health-conscious consumers, use social media') without identifying the mainstream-vs-DTC segmentation distinction or quantifying the price gap.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: This is a new product launch in a fast-growing but still relatively small segment. I want to make sure Wheaties Protein has a distinct reason to exist — not just '20 grams of protein in a cereal box.' I'll use STP to define the specific target and positioning, 3C's to map the competitive landscape, and 4P's to design the execution. Two minutes to structure, then I'll walk through it.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: A couple of quick questions. First, taste validation — has Wheaties Protein been taste-tested against Magic Spoon and against regular Wheaties, and where does it land? Taste drives repeat purchase in cereal more than anything. Second, is the 20 grams of protein achievable at a standard one-ounce serving size, or does it require a larger serving that increases calorie count significantly? That's a common issue with high-protein cereals.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Target Segment
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with who this is actually for. The protein cereal market is driven by fitness-oriented adults who are already on high-protein diets — Magic Spoon has captured a lot of that consumer with Instagram and DTC. But that's a relatively small, premium-skewing group. General Mills' real opportunity is the much larger 'mainstream active adult' — the 30-year-old who goes to the gym three times a week, shops at Walmart or Target, wants more protein in their diet but isn't going to pay $13.99 for a box of cereal online. Magic Spoon doesn't reach this consumer. Wheaties Protein, at $6.99 in the cereal aisle, does.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Positioning
sampleResponse.script: The Wheaties brand's 'Breakfast of Champions' positioning is the biggest asset here. It already signals sports and performance — but it skews nostalgic and slightly older. The positioning for Wheaties Protein should be 'accessible performance nutrition' — the high-protein breakfast for the everyday athlete, not the elite or the Instagram fitness influencer. This is a white space between Magic Spoon's premium niche and regular Wheaties' sports nostalgia. I'd also put a current Olympian or mainstream athlete on the box — not a social media fitness personality, which is Magic Spoon's territory.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — 4P's Execution
sampleResponse.script: On the four P's. Product: confirm 20 grams of protein is deliverable at standard serving size — if the calorie count balloons, the health-conscious buyer will notice and repeat purchase will suffer. Price: $6.99 is the right call — it's $7 cheaper than Magic Spoon and signals accessible performance rather than premium niche. Place: this is General Mills' decisive advantage. Ninety-two percent ACV means Wheaties Protein hits Walmart, Target, and Kroger shelves simultaneously in a way Magic Spoon can never match. Place it in the cereal aisle at eye level, not in the specialty protein section — drive impulse trial from existing cereal shoppers, not just protein seekers. Promotion: sampling at gyms, marathons, and athletic events is the trial driver. The Wheaties heritage makes sponsoring sporting events natural and credible.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My recommendation: launch Wheaties Protein targeting the mainstream active adult at Walmart and Target scale, priced at $6.99. Do not try to out-Magic Spoon Magic Spoon on Instagram or DTC — that's a fight General Mills cannot win authentically. Win instead on mass distribution, accessible pricing, and the Wheaties performance heritage. Put a current Olympian on the box, sample at athletic events, and place in the cereal aisle. Year-one success metric: a 60-day repeat rate of at least 25%, which would confirm that the taste and protein experience lives up to the packaging promise. If repeat is below 20%, investigate taste first before expanding distribution further.

title: Dove Men+Care Entering the Body Wash for Kids Market
prompt: Unilever is evaluating whether to launch a Dove Kids Care body wash and shampoo line for children ages 3-12, priced at $7.99 for 18oz (vs. Johnson's Baby at $5.99 and Suave Kids at $3.99). The children's bath products market is $1.8B growing 5% annually. Dove's equity in 'gentle' and 'moisturizing' has strong resonance with mothers. What launch framework should be applied, and what are the biggest risks?
context: The Dove brand is currently adult-focused. Johnson's Baby (J&J) holds 38% of the children's bath category and is entrenched from birth. Suave Kids holds 22% on value positioning with licensed characters. The 'clean ingredient' segment for kids (Mustela, Babyganics) is growing 19% annually. Consumer research shows 71% of mothers say they research ingredients 'very carefully' for children's products. Dove's existing consumer base (women 25-45) heavily overlaps with the mother target for this launch.
whyThisFramework: Market Entry Framework frames this as a new product (kids' formulation) entering an existing market — a product line extension. STP targets 'ingredient-aware millennial mothers' as the primary segment, distinct from Johnson's heritage users and Suave's value buyers. 4P's executes with premium-but-not-luxury pricing and marketing through mom-influencer channels.
keyConsiderations: Dove's 'gentleness' equity is directly transferable to children's care — credible brand stretch
Johnson's 'from birth' trust is almost unassailable — target the 3-7 age cohort instead
Ingredient transparency (no parabens, no sulfates) is table stakes for millennial mothers
Licensing characters (Marvel, Disney) could accelerate trial in a category where kids influence the purchase
clarifyingQuestions: Is Dove Kids Care formulated with specific child-safe ingredients that Dove can claim are distinct from its adult line — specifically sulfate-free and paraben-free?
Is the target the 3-12 age range broadly, or does Unilever plan to sub-segment — for example, a 3-7 toddler line and a 7-12 'big kids' line?
What is the planned retail channel — mass (Walmart/Target) only, or also specialty baby retailers like Buy Buy Baby or health stores like Whole Foods?
Has Dove tested the brand extension with mothers in focus groups — specifically, do mothers perceive Dove as credible for children, or do they associate Dove solely with adult personal care?

subIssues: Does Dove's 'gentle and moisturizing' equity transfer credibly to children's care in consumer perception, or does the adult positioning create a barrier?
Is there a meaningful segment of millennial mothers who trust Dove as a brand and would switch their children from Johnson's or Suave if Dove offered a clean-ingredient alternative?
What ingredient claim can Dove make that Johnson's and Suave cannot — sulfate-free, paraben-free, dermatologist-tested for kids?
Can Dove occupy the 'premium clean' space between Johnson's mid-market and Mustela's $14.99 luxury, without getting squeezed from both sides?
Is the 3-7 year old cohort — where parents are transitioning away from Johnson's Baby — the most accessible entry point given Johnson's 'from birth' entrenchment?
How does Dove attract kids' influence over the purchase when Suave Kids uses Disney and Marvel character licensing that Dove does not have?
What is the right channel mix — mass retail for volume, specialty baby for credibility, or Target specifically given its overlap with Dove's core demographic?
Is $7.99 the right price point — it's $2 above Johnson's and $4 above Suave, but $7 below Mustela — and will the clean-ingredient positioning justify the premium for the median millennial mother?
How does Unilever drive trial — in-store sampling, mom-influencer seeding, or pediatric dermatologist endorsement?

stepByStep.title: Validate Brand Extension Credibility
stepByStep.detail: Consumer research must confirm that Dove's 'gentle' and 'moisturizing' equity transfers to children's care perception. The Dove brand is adult-associated — request focus group data specifically testing mother receptivity. If mothers see Dove as credible for children, proceed. If not, consider a sub-brand architecture (Dove Baby Care by Dove) to bridge the gap.

stepByStep.title: Target the Right Age Cohort
stepByStep.detail: Johnson's Baby has 38% share and is entrenched from birth through infancy. Do not compete there. The 3-7 age cohort is the natural transition point where parents begin moving away from Johnson's Baby formulas. Target this cohort with an 'ages 3+, gentle and clean' positioning that acknowledges the child is past infancy without requiring a full brand relaunch.

stepByStep.title: Establish the Ingredient Differentiator
stepByStep.detail: Seventy-one percent of mothers research ingredients carefully. Dove Kids Care must be sulfate-free, paraben-free, and dermatologist-tested for children. This is table stakes against Mustela but a meaningful upgrade vs. Johnson's and Suave. Feature the ingredient list prominently on the front panel — ingredient transparency is marketing in this category.

stepByStep.title: 4P's — Price, Place, Promotion
stepByStep.detail: Price: $7.99 at mass retail is correct — it's the 'affordable premium' that separates Dove from the value players without crossing into Mustela luxury. Place: lead with Target (overlaps directly with Dove's 25-45 female core demographic) and Walmart. Promotion: mom-influencer partnerships on Instagram and TikTok, with pediatric dermatologist seeding as credibility anchor. Kids influence: colorful packaging and appealing scents without requiring character licensing.

stepByStep.title: Recommendation
stepByStep.detail: Launch Dove Kids Care targeting mothers of 3-7 year olds, positioned as the clean-ingredient gentle alternative to Johnson's. Lead with Target distribution, mom-influencer partnerships, and front-of-pack ingredient transparency. Price at $7.99. Avoid competing directly with Johnson's in the infant segment — own the 'transition to clean' moment at ages 3-4.

keyTerms.definition: Launching a new product under an existing brand name — Dove Kids Care extends the Dove adult personal care brand into children's bath products, borrowing its 'gentle and moisturizing' equity

Creating a named variation under the parent brand (e.g., 'Dove Baby Care') to signal a distinct formulation or target while maintaining parent brand equity — useful when the parent brand's adult associations might create credibility questions

Prominently disclosing what a product does and does not contain (sulfate-free, paraben-free, dermatologist-tested); a purchase decision driver for 71% of millennial mothers in the children's bath category

In children's products, both the parent (primary buyer) and the child (influencer) affect the purchase — mothers evaluate safety and ingredients while children respond to packaging, scent, and character association

Positioned above mass market (Johnson's $5.99, Suave $3.99) but below luxury (Mustela $14.99) — Dove's $7.99 targets mothers who want cleaner ingredients but won't pay European skincare prices for bath wash

interviewerExpectations: Strong answers recognize immediately that Johnson's Baby cannot be beaten head-on in the infant segment and pivot to the 3-7 age cohort as the entry point. They identify ingredient transparency as the category differentiator and Target as the strategic retail partner. Weak answers propose a generic kids' bath launch without segmenting by age cohort or identifying which specific mothers Dove can realistically convert.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: This is a product line extension — a new kids formulation entering an established market. Before recommending a launch plan, I need to identify which specific segment of mothers Dove can realistically win, because Johnson's and Suave have very different and entrenched positions. I'll use a Market Entry Framework to evaluate the opportunity, STP to pick the right target and positioning, and 4P's to design the launch. A quick moment to structure.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: A few quick questions. First, has Dove validated with mother focus groups that the brand feels credible for children — specifically, do mothers see 'Dove' and think 'gentle for my kid' or do they think 'that's my body wash'? Second, is the formulation sulfate-free and paraben-free — because ingredient transparency is non-negotiable for the millennial mother segment? And third, is the Target partnership already confirmed, or is this general mass retail?

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Market Entry Assessment
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with the strategic question: can Dove win in this market? Johnson's Baby holds 38% share and is entrenched from birth — parents choose Johnson's for their first child, use it through infancy, and the habit sticks. Dove is not going to displace that in the zero-to-two age group. But the 3-to-7 year old cohort is where parents begin to reconsider — they're past the baby stage, and a growing segment of millennial mothers is actively looking for cleaner-ingredient alternatives. The 'clean ingredient' segment — Mustela, Babyganics — is growing 19% per year. That's the beachhead. Dove doesn't need to fight Johnson's on its home turf. It needs to own the transition moment.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Target & Positioning
sampleResponse.script: The target is millennial mothers of 3 to 7 year olds who are ingredient-aware. Seventy-one percent of mothers research ingredients carefully for their children's products. This consumer wants clean — sulfate-free, paraben-free — but she's not going to spend $14.99 for Mustela. She's shopping Target, looking for something that feels premium but is priced reasonably. Dove's 'gentle and moisturizing' brand equity is a direct credibility transfer to this use case. The positioning: 'The clean-ingredient bath care for kids, from a brand mothers already trust.' Dove is the bridge between Johnson's mass familiarity and Mustela's expensive clean-label positioning.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — 4P's Execution
sampleResponse.script: On the four P's. Product: the formulation must be sulfate-free, paraben-free, and dermatologist-tested for children — these are table stakes for the ingredient-aware mother, not differentiators. Feature them prominently on the front of the pack. Price: $7.99 is the right call. It's $2 above Johnson's, which signals 'cleaner and better,' and $7 below Mustela, which signals 'accessible premium, not indulgent.' Place: lead with Target, which over-indexes with Dove's core 25-45 female demographic and already carries the adult Dove line — the in-store adjacency helps. Add Walmart for volume coverage. Promotion: invest in mom-influencer partnerships on Instagram and TikTok — this is where the ingredient-aware millennial mother gets her product recommendations. Seed with pediatric dermatologists for clinical credibility. Colorful, fun packaging that kids respond to without requiring character licensing.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My recommendation: yes, launch Dove Kids Care, targeting mothers of 3 to 7 year olds who are ingredient-aware and actively transitioning away from Johnson's Baby. Do not compete in the infant segment — that's Johnson's fortress. Own the 'transition to clean' moment at age three. Lead with Target distribution and mom-influencer marketing, front the ingredient story prominently on pack, price at $7.99, and ensure the formulation is sulfate-free and paraben-free before launch — that's the baseline expectation for the target consumer. Year-one success metric: trial rate at Target locations in the first 90 days, and a 60-day repeat rate above 30%, which would confirm that ingredient-aware mothers are making it a household staple rather than a one-time experiment.

title: Bud Light Launching a Non-Alcoholic Beer
prompt: AB InBev is evaluating the launch of Bud Light Zero, a 0% ABV beer priced at $9.99 for a 6-pack (vs. $10.99 for Bud Light regular). The non-alcoholic beer category is growing 35% annually and reached $510M in the US in the prior year. Athletic Brewing Co. holds 35% of the NA beer market and is growing 50% YoY. Heineken 0.0 holds 18%. How would you approach the launch strategy?
context: Bud Light is the #1 volume beer in the US. The non-alcoholic beer buyer skews toward health-conscious adults 25-40 who are 'sober curious' or 'mindful drinkers.' Bud Light's core demographic is sports fans 21-35. Athletic Brewing positions on craft/performance ('Brew Free or Die'). The social occasion of drinking — holding a beer at a party or game — is a key purchase driver for NA beer. Bud Light's distribution is nearly universal.
whyThisFramework: STP determines whether Bud Light Zero targets existing Bud Light drinkers (who want to drink less) or NA beer adopters (who may not drink Bud Light). This changes everything about the positioning and creative. 3C's maps Athletic Brewing as a differentiated craft competitor. 4P's executes with Bud Light's distribution advantage and sports occasion marketing.
keyConsiderations: Bud Light's mass distribution is a massive advantage — Athletic Brewing cannot match shelf presence
Social occasion is the key insight: NA beer is about being included, not being excluded
Pricing below regular Bud Light ($0.00 premium for NA) is counterintuitive — most NA brands carry a premium
Athletic Brewing's craft positioning is a different segment than Bud Light's mass positioning — don't fight directly
clarifyingQuestions: Who is the primary target for Bud Light Zero — existing Bud Light drinkers who want a no-alcohol option for some occasions, or consumers who have already converted to NA beer and are currently buying Athletic Brewing or Heineken 0.0?
Is the $9.99 price point firm, or is there flexibility to price at parity with regular Bud Light ($10.99) to avoid signaling lower quality?
Is the plan to place Bud Light Zero in the existing beer aisle alongside regular Bud Light, or in the NA section — and does this vary by retailer?
Has Bud Light done taste testing against Athletic Brewing's Run Wild IPA and Heineken 0.0, and how does it score on taste and drinkability?

subIssues: Is the core buyer a Bud Light loyalist who wants a zero-alcohol option for driving nights, mornings after, or dry months — or a new-to-Bud-Light consumer who has already adopted NA?
Is the primary purchase occasion social (sporting events, parties where NA beer allows inclusion) or individual (personal health or dry month goals)?
Does the 'sober curious' movement represent a temporary wellness trend or a sustained behavioral shift that justifies long-term SKU investment?
Is Athletic Brewing's 'craft performance' consumer the same as Bud Light's sports-fan mass consumer — and if not, is there actually direct competition?
Does Heineken 0.0's established retail presence make it a more direct competitor than Athletic Brewing in the mass grocery channel?
Can Bud Light Zero credibly occupy the 'inclusive social beverage' positioning — being the beer you hold at the game when you're not drinking — without craft credibility?
Does Bud Light's near-universal distribution translate to shelf placement alongside its regular beer — and can it command prime fridge space in the NA section?
At $9.99 vs. $10.99 for regular Bud Light, is the pricing counter-positioning clear ('NA costs less, try it') or confusing ('why is it cheaper — is it lower quality')?
What is the in-venue (stadium, bar, restaurant) distribution strategy — draft handles for NA are rare but represent a significant social occasion opportunity?

stepByStep.title: Define the Core Target (STP)
stepByStep.detail: Primary target: existing Bud Light drinkers, 22-38 year old sports fans, who want a zero-alcohol option for specific occasions — driving nights, morning events, or moderating during a night out. This is NOT Athletic Brewing's target (fitness-first, craft-curious 28-40 year old). These are two different people with different needs. Bud Light Zero should not try to win the Athletic Brewing consumer.

stepByStep.title: Identify the Occasion
stepByStep.detail: The primary purchase driver for NA beer is social inclusion — holding a beer at a party or game without drinking alcohol. For Bud Light drinkers, the occasion is 'I'm at the Super Bowl party, I'm driving, I still want to hold a Bud Light.' This occasion-based positioning frames Bud Light Zero as 'the same, minus the alcohol' — not a compromise, but a choice.

stepByStep.title: Competitive Separation from Athletic Brewing
stepByStep.detail: Athletic Brewing targets craft beer lovers and fitness enthusiasts with 'performance sobriety' messaging. Do not fight there — Bud Light has no craft credibility. Bud Light Zero's positioning is the opposite: mass-market, social, accessible. The tagline concept: 'Same great taste. Zero percent.' Not performance, not craft — just Bud Light without the alcohol.

stepByStep.title: 4P's — Price, Place, Promotion
stepByStep.detail: Price: $9.99 is fine, though the $1 below regular Bud Light risks a quality signal. Consider matching at $10.99 or framing as 'same price for a lighter choice.' Place: stock Bud Light Zero on the shelf immediately next to regular Bud Light — not just in the NA section. This reinforces 'same choice, different occasion' rather than relegating it to a specialty shelf. Promotion: sports occasion marketing — NFL, March Madness, sporting event sponsorships. The creative: Bud Light drinkers making the choice to go zero on specific occasions without missing out.

stepByStep.title: Recommendation
stepByStep.detail: Launch Bud Light Zero targeting existing Bud Light drinkers for social occasions, positioned as 'the same Bud Light experience, zero alcohol.' Distribute alongside regular Bud Light — not only in the NA section. Lead promotion with sports occasion marketing and in-venue (stadium) distribution. Do not try to compete with Athletic Brewing's craft/performance positioning — own the mass social occasion instead. Pricing at $10.99 matching regular Bud Light avoids the quality signal issue and normalizes the choice.

keyTerms.definition: The percentage of alcohol in a beverage — Bud Light Zero is 0% ABV, making it legally non-alcoholic; this is the defining product characteristic for NA beer positioning and retail placement
A consumer movement of adults who choose to reduce or eliminate alcohol consumption for health or lifestyle reasons without fully identifying as sober; the primary driver of NA beer category growth
Targeting consumers not by demographics but by the specific use moment — NA beer's key occasion is 'social drinking situations where the consumer wants to participate without drinking alcohol'

The competitive advantage created by Bud Light's near-universal retail presence — Athletic Brewing and Heineken 0.0 cannot match Bud Light's ability to place Bud Light Zero on shelves next to the regular product at mass scale
A unique product variant tracked in retail inventory — Bud Light Zero is a new SKU requiring shelf space, retail buyer approval, and warehouse logistics separate from regular Bud Light

interviewerExpectations: Strong answers identify that Bud Light Zero's primary target is existing Bud Light drinkers on specific social occasions — not the Athletic Brewing craft consumer — and use that insight to drive a completely different positioning and channel strategy. They call out the distribution moat as the decisive competitive advantage and the pricing signal risk at $9.99. Weak answers treat this as a generic NA beer launch without identifying the Bud Light loyalist vs. Athletic Brewing consumer distinction.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: The most important strategic decision here isn't the product or the price — it's who Bud Light Zero is actually for. If it's for existing Bud Light drinkers who want a zero-alcohol option on certain occasions, that's a completely different launch than if it's trying to win Athletic Brewing's craft-performance consumer. I'll use STP to answer that question first, 3C's to map the competitive dynamics, and 4P's to design the execution. Can I have a minute to organize my thinking?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: A couple of quick questions. First, is the intent to target existing Bud Light drinkers — people who already love Bud Light and want a zero option for specific nights — or to attract new-to-Bud-Light consumers who have already adopted the NA category through Athletic Brewing or Heineken 0.0? These are very different people with different needs. Second, is the plan to shelve Bud Light Zero next to regular Bud Light in the beer aisle, or primarily in the dedicated non-alcoholic section? That placement decision signals who the product is for.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Target Segment
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with the target. Bud Light's core consumer is a sports fan, 21 to 35, who drinks Bud Light at games, parties, and tailgates. The NA beer category overall skews health-conscious adults 25 to 40 — some of whom are 'sober curious,' some are athletes, some are just moderating. Athletic Brewing has captured the craft-performance end of that spectrum — the person who does triathlons and wants a beer that fits their lifestyle identity. That is not Bud Light's consumer. Bud Light's consumer is the person at the Super Bowl party who is driving home, or who is on a dry January, or who just doesn't want to drink tonight but absolutely wants to hold a Bud Light and feel like part of the game. That's the target. Social inclusion is the job to be done — not performance, not craft credibility.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Positioning vs. Competitors
sampleResponse.script: Given that target, Bud Light Zero should not try to compete with Athletic Brewing's 'Brew Free or Die' performance sobriety positioning. Bud Light has no craft credibility to lend. Instead, the positioning is completely different: 'Same great taste. Zero percent.' Not a compromise — a choice. The creative concept: Bud Light drinkers at a tailgate, at a game, at a backyard party, choosing Zero for the occasion and not missing out on anything. This is about inclusion, not deprivation. Heineken 0.0 is a closer direct competitor in the mass grocery channel — but Heineken's awareness is lower among Bud Light's core sports-fan demographic, which gives Bud Light Zero a real opening.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Distribution as the Decisive Advantage
sampleResponse.script: Bud Light's near-universal distribution is the moat. Athletic Brewing cannot match it. Heineken 0.0 has good distribution but not Bud Light's presence. The key execution decision: place Bud Light Zero directly next to regular Bud Light on the shelf, in the main beer aisle, not just in the specialty NA section. This placement communicates 'same choice, different occasion' — a Bud Light drinker browsing the beer aisle sees Zero right next to their regular pack and makes the switch naturally. If it's only in the NA section, it becomes a specialty product for people who have already decided to go NA, which is a smaller audience.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Price & Promotion
sampleResponse.script: On price: I'd actually push back on the $9.99 versus $10.99 regular Bud Light framing. One dollar cheaper for the zero-alcohol version risks signaling lower quality — 'they removed the alcohol and a dollar.' I'd recommend matching at $10.99 or pricing at parity, framing the choice as an equal but different option. Promotion: sports occasion marketing is the natural channel. NFL sponsorships, March Madness, stadium distribution where fans who are driving still want to hold something that looks and feels like a Bud Light. The creative hook: 'Zero never felt so cold.' Normalize the occasion without making it about sacrifice.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My recommendation: launch Bud Light Zero targeting existing Bud Light drinkers for social occasions where they want to participate without drinking alcohol. Position it as 'the same Bud Light, zero percent' — not craft, not performance, just inclusion. Place it next to regular Bud Light in the beer aisle as the priority, with secondary placement in the NA section. Price it at $10.99, matching regular Bud Light to avoid a quality signal issue. Lead promotion with sports occasion marketing — NFL, college football, stadium distribution — where Bud Light's brand equity is strongest. Do not try to compete with Athletic Brewing on craft or performance; own the mass social occasion instead. Year-one success metric: trial rate among existing Bud Light household buyers and sell-through velocity at sporting event venues, which are the proving ground for the social occasion positioning.
